name: Diagram Brain
purpose: Create and revise diagram specifications for JSXGraph, Penrose, and Plotly without mixing diagram rendering rules into question logic.
owns: geometricConstruction, network, and setDiagram Penrose substance
mustNotOwn: page-level spacing except diagram alignment and size
rules: Use graph2d or graph3d for coordinate graphs and relations.

When emitting diagram actions, use only supported graphConfig.type values: graph2d, vector2d, graph3d, image, geometricConstruction, network, setDiagram, or statsChart. Do not invent alternate type names such as binomialChart, vennDiagram, networkGraph, or coordinateVector.

Assistant-authored high-level diagram blocks must normally be shaped as { graphConfig: { type: ... }, diagramAlign?: ... }. For source-faithful scalar-product ray diagrams, the assistant may instead use the compact { vectorRayDiagram: { vectors, segmentLabels?, angleMarkers? }, diagramAlign?: ... } builder, which Mauth expands into ordinary vector2d graphConfig metadata. Never put type, data, options, or metadata directly on the diagram block, and never use config as an alias for graphConfig. The high-level authoring tools reject these shapes before applying changes so the model can repair them.

If a diagram action returns validationIssues, repair the exact graphConfig field paths and retry once. Common repair paths include statsChart data arrays, vector2d metadata vectors, graph2d ranges/functions/features, setDiagram set/region names, Penrose relationship point names, image data.src, and graph3d metadata.view3d.

Assistant commit preflight blocks accidental diagram removal for solution, marking, wording, and diagram-addition edits. Use explicit question replacement with diagrams intentionally omitted only when the teacher asks to remove or replace diagrams.

For focused assistant requests such as add a diagram to Question 1, prefer the exposed high-level diagram tool. In the OpenAI provider this may be named mauth_make_diagram_for_question; it maps to mauth.author.addDiagram. It can target a question by questionNumber or questionId, insert the diagram before the student space, replace an existing top-level diagram by diagramId, and accept a real renderer-specific graphConfig.

mauth.author.addDiagram is only for existing questions. When the teacher attaches a source question or says to add the attached question to the test, the diagram belongs inside the mauth.question.upsert source-question payload. If addDiagram validation reports that the target question does not exist, treat that as wrong-tool routing and switch to question upsert/source conversion rather than retrying addDiagram.

Do not use standardDiagram recipe names for assistant-authored diagrams. The assistant should make an intelligent renderer choice, request/load the relevant brain guidance, then provide the appropriate native graphConfig.

The high-level assistant authoring tools run deterministic intent validation for obvious diagram families before applying edits. If circle/tangent/chord theorem geometry is sent as graph2d, a scalar-product ray diagram is sent as network/graph2d/Penrose, a coordinate vector is sent as Penrose geometry/network, a probability chart is sent as graph2d, or a Venn diagram is sent as the wrong renderer, the tool rejects graphConfig.type with a repairable issue. Repair by switching to the expected native renderer, not by weakening the validation.

After a diagram exists, mauth.preview.inspect reports question.diagrams[].warnings from the shared diagram inspection layer and rendered preview warnings when the preview pane is mounted. Treat warnings such as diagram-renderer-mismatch, image-diagram-missing-source, scalar-product-vector-labels-missing, scalar-product-angle-marker-missing, stats-chart-probabilities-not-normalised, stats-chart-relative-frequency-mode-missing, vector2d-labels-missing, set-diagram-shading-missing, set-diagram-counts-missing, rendered-diagram-failed, rendered-diagram-label-collision, rendered-diagram-clipped, rendered-response-space-outline-missing, and penrose-* semantic warnings as repair targets before saying the diagram is done.

After a successful high-level diagram edit, the assistant may receive semanticReview.required with a compact postEditInspection artifact. Compare the teacher request, visible question text, part text, and diagram summary before reporting success. If the diagram payload does not match the question's mathematical object, repair it with the appropriate high-level tool. This model-level artifact review is preferred over accumulating one-off deterministic rules for every possible graph/question mismatch.

The shared diagram inspector is semantic as well as structural. If it says a statsChart probability chart is not normalised, fix probabilities so they sum to 1. If it says relativeFrequency or manualProbabilities mode is missing, change the statsChart data mode/y-axis mode rather than changing wording. If it says vector2d-labels-missing, add the named vector metadata with name/start/components. If it says setDiagram shading/counts are missing, set the relevant region shaded or add numeric region/set/universe count labels. If it says graph2d is missing a tangent/region/asymptote feature, add the requested graph feature or explain only if the teacher wanted a blank student grid.

High-level diagram edits are automatically post-inspected by the frontend adapter. It waits for the browser preview to repaint when mounted, then inspects fresh rendered metrics. If a diagram edit returns validationIssues with a targetId or diagramId, retry mauth.author.addDiagram once with that diagramId and a corrected graphConfig so the existing bad diagram is replaced rather than appending another diagram. If the warning is layout-specific, repair the adjacent answer/solution-space layout rather than changing the mathematical diagram.

Assistant commit preflight uses the same shared diagram inspection for changed diagrams. It blocks hard failures such as wrong renderer, missing image source, missing scalar-product labels/angle markers/hidden-axis settings for vectors used in dot products, and Penrose circle geometry that does not declare the tangent/chord/circle relationships described by the question.

When converting source papers, preserve intentional graph alignment from the source. If a graph is left-aligned in the original, set the diagram alignment left instead of centering it unless that causes a clear layout problem.
For graph2d and vector2d coordinate axes, the x and y axis-letter labels are MathJax-rendered labels. Keep them as mathematical labels rather than relying on JSXGraph plain-text axis names.

For graph3d diagrams, preserve any stored camera view in graphConfig.metadata.view3d. The interactive preview writes azimuth, elevation, and bank there after the teacher rotates the diagram, and print/export preview should rebuild from those values.

For graph3d diagrams, use MathJax-rendered labels with the same 10 pt-equivalent graph label sizing as other diagrams. Keep the default JSXGraph rear/front 3D planes and plane grid hidden unless the task explicitly needs a 3D grid or plane.

Do not treat graph3d as a 2D function graph. A graph3d config should normally store type, widthPx, heightPx, and metadata.view3d camera values, not graph2d functions, 2D axis controls, or vector2d metadata.

Use the saved DIAGRAM AUDIT GALLERY test as the visual regression fixture when changing diagram renderers, chart defaults, or diagram editor controls. With the API and web app running, pnpm smoke:diagram-gallery captures repeatable screenshots for the key renderer examples.
Use image diagrams for teacher-uploaded image files. The current editor stores uploaded images as data URLs, but future file-management work should move large images into asset storage.

When a teacher attaches a screenshot, PDF, Word document, or text-like source that contains or describes a diagram, first decide whether it should be recreated as a native Mauth renderer. Use graph2d/vector2d/statsChart/setDiagram/geometricConstruction/graph3d when the diagram is mathematical and editable; use image only when the attachment itself is the intended bitmap or the diagram cannot yet be recreated accurately.

When a screenshot/source prompt explicitly says to enter the diagram underneath the question, create a native diagram block in the same high-level question payload. Do not replace the source diagram with explanatory prose. Put the diagram before the structured parts when the source layout has the diagram above the parts.

Use geometricConstruction for static geometry, angle, circle theorem, tangent, parallel, perpendicular, and construction diagrams. If the prompt is relationship-based rather than coordinate-based, prefer Penrose/geometricConstruction over graph2d. For assistant-authored geometricConstruction diagrams, native means supported Penrose Substance in graphConfig.options.substanceSource. Do not invent a second geometry language unless the UI control itself supplies structured data.

Visible geometric diagram labels must match the question statement. Auxiliary points needed only to construct a circle, radius, tangent, or relationship should be hidden with an invisible label and HidePoint unless the question explicitly names that point.

For tangent-parallel-chord diagrams, write supported Penrose Substance directly: declare a hidden auxiliary centre plus the visible question points, for example Point centre, A, B, C; Circle omega; Line tangentA; NamedSegment AB, AC, BC; Label centre $\,$; HidePoint(centre); then use CircleThrough(omega, centre, A), OnCircle(B, omega), OnCircle(C, omega), Tangent(tangentA, omega, A), Segment(AB, A, B), Segment(AC, A, C), Segment(BC, B, C), and ParallelToSegment(tangentA, B, C). Do not visibly label a point such as O unless the question names it. Do not use a canned diagram recipe.

For assistant-authored geometricConstruction diagrams with graphConfig.options.substanceSource, graphConfig.data may be omitted or left as an empty object. Do not generate parallel structured objects/relationships unless the UI helper path specifically needs them.

For Penrose geometry Substance, declare named sides with NamedSegment AB, AC, BC and draw them with Segment(AB, A, B). Do not write Segment AB, AC as a type declaration. Use OnCircle(P, circle) for points on a circle, not On(P, circle). Use ParallelToSegment(lineName, B, C) or PerpendicularToSegment(lineName, B, C) only when constraining a drawn line relative to a segment, not for drawing a right-angle marker.

For Penrose labels, use the supported label syntax exactly. Every predicate call must use parentheses and commas, for example VectorSegment(OA, O, A); never write declaration-like predicate syntax such as VectorSegment OA O A. To label a point, write Label A $A$ or Label A $\mathbf{a}$ directly on the existing point name; do not invent LabelsPoint or a separate point label variable. To label a segment or vector length, write Label lenA $2\ \text{units}$ and then LabelsSegment(lenA, O, A) or LabelsSegment(lenA, OA); do not write LabelSegment. To draw a segment or vector, use Segment(name, A, B) or VectorSegment(name, A, B), not Connect(...). To show collinearity, first declare Line lineName, then use LineThrough(lineName, A, B) plus On(P, lineName) only when incidence is essential; do not invent Collinear(...). To label an angle, always declare a label such as Label angleCD $45^\circ$, then call LabelsAngle(angleCD, C, O, D). Do not put raw TeX inside LabelsAngle and do not write a four-argument LabelsAngle with the display value as the fourth argument.

For Penrose rays, declare Ray rayA and draw it with RayFrom(rayA, O, A). Do not write Ray(rayA, O, A). For arrowed vector rays from a common origin, prefer NamedSegment OA plus VectorSegment(OA, O, A) so the arrowhead is part of the vector segment.

Use vector2d for coordinate-accurate vector diagrams and source-faithful no-axis vector/ray diagrams: vectors with numeric components, common or varied start points, axes when needed, hidden axes when the source has no coordinate grid, vector addition/subtraction, scalar-product ray diagrams, and Cartesian-plane labels. The vector2d renderer uses the same clean major/minor grid, axes, arrows, tick spacing, and label styling as ordinary 2D function graphs.

For scalar-product source diagrams showing labelled rays from one common initial point with magnitudes and angle markers but no Cartesian axes, prefer the vectorRayDiagram builder in high-level assistant tools. Provide vectors with stable id/name such as a, b, c, d, either length plus angleDeg or explicit components, optional labelX/labelY for source-like label placement, lengthLabel for magnitude text, and angleMarkers with from/to vector ids for right angles and labels such as 45^\circ. Angles are standard degrees: 0 points right, 90 points up, 180 points left, and 270 points down. Mauth expands this into vector2d with showAxes:false, showGrid:false, showAxisLabels:false, showAxisNumbers:false, equalScale:true, labelStyle custom, metadata.vector2d.vectors, segmentLabels, and angleMarkers. When hand-writing raw vector2d graphConfig, follow the same metadata rules manually. Segment labels and angle-marker labels can use labelX and labelY for manual placement and are draggable after render. Do not use network for these scalar-product ray diagrams; network is for conceptual network/link diagrams. Do not use geometricConstruction/Penrose for these source ray diagrams, because auto-layout can move the rays and break source fidelity. Use geometricConstruction/Penrose for theorem geometry, circle geometry, tangent/chord diagrams, and ruler-style construction diagrams.

For vector2d authoring, prefer the structured presets before hand-building every field: single vector, two vectors from origin, vector addition triangle, or component-guide solution. The presets are just normal graphConfig.metadata.vector2d data, so an AI can still patch individual vectors, label positions, and axis bounds afterwards.

Use network as a schematic Penrose network diagram, not as a scalar-product or vector-geometry diagram. It is for directed or undirected links between named nodes where layout is conceptual rather than to scale.
Use setDiagram for Venn and set-region diagrams.

For Venn region labels, prefer school set notation such as A \cap B', A \cap B, A' \cap B, and (A \cup B)' rather than slash-style set difference unless the teacher explicitly asks for setminus notation.

Place Venn region labels cleanly: when set-total side tabs are present, keep the A-total, A-only, intersection, B-only, and B-total values on a common horizontal baseline where practical, but centre each value inside its own visible region or side tab rather than distributing the labels as one evenly spaced row. Put the set labels A and B just outside the top of their circles rather than inside the circle regions, and put the outside-region label near the lower-right of the universal rectangle.

For two-set Venn diagrams, shade standard regions by setting the relevant region data to shaded: true or by using the Penrose predicates ShadeLeftOnly(A, B), ShadeIntersection(A, B), ShadeRightOnly(A, B), and ShadeOutside(U, A, B).
When a Venn question asks for a shaded region, shade the actual region rather than relying only on a text label that says what should be shaded.
Venn/set labels should render as plain dark mathematical text without a white halo or outline. Do not add text stroke behind labels just because a region is shaded.

For Venn diagrams that show element counts, region labels may be numbers; use universe countLabel/count/total for the universal-set total box and set countLabel/count/total for the small set-total badges.

Use Venn count badges sparingly. The preferred school style is a small square badge attached to the top-right corner of the universal rectangle for n(U), total elements, or U when explicitly wanted, and small arc-only semicircle side tabs attached to the outside edge of the relevant set circle for set totals such as n(A) and n(B). These side tabs should be arcs only, with no straight chord line, and the count should be visually centred inside the semicircle tab rather than outside the diagram or on a region boundary. Leave these badges out when the question does not need that information.

For Venn/set diagrams, keep long set-notation region labels spread away from circle outlines. When the regions are numeric element counts, centre A-only, intersection, and B-only counts in their own visible regions, using a shared horizontal baseline when it does not pull labels onto circle boundaries.

For common two-set Venn authoring, use the structured setDiagram controls and presets: set-notation labels, count regions, count regions with total badges, and one-shaded-region presets. Keep ordinary labels, optional U/A/B totals, region labels/counts, and shading as separate structured fields.

Venn labels and count-badge text should use the same 10 pt-equivalent maths sizing as document text and should be compensated against both diagram scalePercent and the renderer's default display scale so changing the diagram scale does not also scale the label text.

The custom two-set Venn renderer treats scalePercent 100 as the standard classroom size and displays the 420 by 300 SVG canvas at 80% by default. This makes the Venn geometry about 20% smaller while keeping labels at document text size. Increase scale only when a larger diagram is genuinely needed.

If a future Venn task explicitly needs A-only or B-only tab-style placeholders rather than ordinary in-region labels, use the same small semicircle attachment convention on the relevant left-only or right-only region. Otherwise, put A-only, intersection, B-only, and outside values as plain labels inside their regions.

Prefer structured set diagram data for ordinary two-set Venn diagrams because the editor exposes labels, counts, total badges, and shaded-region toggles. Use custom Substance only for unusual set diagrams outside those controls.
Use statsChart for histograms, binomial distributions, normal curves, box plots, and statistical data visualisation.
For statsChart histogram/column graph displays, use barType continuous for binned histograms and barType discrete for separate value columns.
For statsChart histogram/column graph displays, use yAxisMode frequency for counts and relativeFrequency for relative frequencies or probability-style bars calculated from raw data.
For statsChart histogram/column graph displays, use yLabelOrientation horizontal when the y-axis label should read across the top-left of the chart instead of rotated vertically.
For exact probability bars that should not be calculated from raw data, use dataMode manualProbabilities with matching xValues and probabilities.
Do not unify JSXGraph, Penrose, and Plotly internals.
Use LaTeX-rendered labels where labels contain mathematical notation.

For LaTeX-rendered diagram labels, follow the same MathJax style contract as text blocks. Keep labels concise; if a large expression must appear inside a fraction numerator or denominator, add explicit \displaystyle to that nested expression.
Keep diagram label font visually consistent with 10 pt test text unless a specific style says otherwise.
For free graph labels, store label positions as editable feature coordinates so labels can be dragged and remain round-trippable.

For solution copies, add small visual annotations when they clarify the worked solution: highlighted intersection points, dashed guide lines to axes, or labelled critical values. Use a contrasting colour such as red only for the solution annotation, not for changing the original diagram meaning.
For graph2d solution annotations, use structured features such as point, label, and line_segment guide lines rather than ad hoc SVG or changing the underlying functions.
Given question diagrams are ordinary shared test modules and should appear in both the student copy and solution copy.
Assistant diagram edits must preserve existing shared diagrams unless the task is a genuine question replacement or the teacher explicitly asks to remove the diagram.
When a diagram is created only for the worked solution, store it as a solution module so it hides from the student copy.

When solution text or answer space should sit beside a given diagram or solution-only diagram, align the diagram left or right and let the renderer create the automatic diagram response slot. Student space appears as one L-shaped side-and-under area; solution content wraps beside the diagram and continues underneath at full width if needed. Treat this as automatic assessment layout, not a separate publishing wrap control.
For Penrose geometry, prefer readable named segments such as Segment(AB, A, B) and EqualLength(AB, AC).

For vector2d, store each vector in graphConfig.metadata.vector2d.vectors with id, name, start [x,y], components [dx,dy], color, and optional showComponents. The id is the stable reference used by segmentLabels.vectorId and angleMarkers.from/to; do not omit it. Set graphConfig.metadata.vector2d.labelStyle to boldLower for labels like \mathbf{a}=\begin{pmatrix}2\\3\end{pmatrix}, arrow for labels like \overrightarrow{AB}=\begin{pmatrix}2\\3\end{pmatrix}, or custom when each vector supplies its own label. Use equalScale:true whenever visual angles, perpendicularity, or source-faithful ray geometry matter. Vector labels, segment labels, and angle labels are draggable in the preview; store manual label positions as labelX and labelY on the vector, segment label, or angle marker, or leave them unset for automatic placement. Use metadata.vector2d.angleMarkers for angle arcs and perpendicular markers: set from/to to vector ids, set rightAngle:true for a square right-angle marker, otherwise use label such as 45^\circ and optional radius/color. Keep showComponents false by default; set it true only in the Annotations controls for a specific question or solution annotation where dotted component guide lines help the reasoning. A vector such as a=(2,3) from the origin should be vector2d, not Penrose.
For network diagrams, use VectorSegment(name, start, end) for directed arrows and Segment(name, start, end) for undirected links.

For a circle through named points, use a hidden auxiliary centre plus CircleThrough(omega, centre, A) and OnCircle(B, omega), OnCircle(C, omega). Do not call CircleThrough with three circumference points, such as CircleThrough(omega, A, B, C).

Prefer structured network data for schematic network diagrams because the editor exposes node rows, node labels, directed/undirected links, and link labels. Use custom Substance only when the network needs unusual Penrose constraints.
Triangle(A, B, C) draws the triangle, but add named Segment(...) declarations when a side needs a label, tick mark, or relationship.
Use EqualLength2 or EqualLength3 for two- or three-tick side marks.
Use AngleMark, AngleMark2, or AngleMark3 for one-, two-, or three-arc angle marks.
Hide point dots or labels only when the mathematical diagram convention requires it.
For graph regions, avoid unnecessary boundary strokes; use fill opacity and clear clipping to the grid.
For statsChart diagrams, use controlled chart styling: black axes and labels, clean grid boundaries, optional fill colour, and fill opacity. Do not rely on Plotly defaults for assessment output.
checks: Diagram type matches the mathematical object.
Attachment-derived diagrams use a native renderer where practical rather than defaulting to a bitmap.
Assistant diagram, solution, and marking edits preserve existing shared diagrams unless the teacher explicitly requested a genuine diagram removal or question replacement.
Labels do not cross important lines where avoidable.
Graph axes, labels, and numbering are readable.
LaTeX-rendered diagram labels follow the shared MathJax display-style convention without becoming oversized or cramped.
Stats chart bins, bars, dataMode, yAxisMode, and probability totals match the statistical interpretation.
Set diagram shaded regions and numeric counts/totals match the described set expression, optional total/tab badges appear only when needed, and region labels stay readable without white outlines.
Penrose Substance is valid for the selected preset.
Solution-only diagram annotations support the reasoning without cluttering or changing the original mathematical object.
Solution-only diagrams and side-by-side diagram text hide correctly on the student copy.
After diagram-renderer changes, the DIAGRAM AUDIT GALLERY opens cleanly and its expected-result notes still match the rendered diagrams.
